or the **Eastern Highlands**, is a [cordillera](/wiki/Cordillera "Cordillera") system in eastern [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") consisting of an expansive collection of [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range"), [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") and [rolling hills](/wiki/Hill "Hill"). It runs roughly parallel to the [east coast of Australia](/wiki/East_coast_of_Australia "East coast of Australia") and forms the [fifth-longest land-based mountain chain in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_mountain_chains_on_Earth "List of longest mountain chains on Earth"), and the longest entirely within a single country. It is [mainland Australia](/wiki/Mainland_Australia "Mainland Australia")'s most substantial [topographic feature](/wiki/Topographic_feature "Topographic feature") and serves as the definitive [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") for the [river systems](/wiki/River_system "River system") in eastern Australia, hence the name.

The **Great Dividing Range**, also known as the **East Australian Cordillera** or the **Eastern Highlands**, is a [cordillera](/wiki/Cordillera "Cordillera") system in eastern [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") consisting of an expansive collection of [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range"), [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") and [rolling hills](/wiki/Hill "Hill"). It runs roughly parallel to the [east coast of Australia](/wiki/East_coast_of_Australia "East coast of Australia") and forms the [fifth-longest land-based mountain chain in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_mountain_chains_on_Earth "List of longest mountain chains on Earth"), and the longest entirely within a single country. It is [mainland Australia](/wiki/Mainland_Australia "Mainland Australia")'s most substantial [topographic feature](/wiki/Topographic_feature "Topographic feature") and serves as the definitive [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") for the [river systems](/wiki/River_system "River system") in eastern Australia, hence the name.

The Great Dividing Range stretches more than 3,500 kilometres (2,175 mi) from [Dauan Island](/wiki/Dauan_Island,_Queensland "Dauan Island, Queensland") in the [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait") off the northern tip of [Cape York Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula"), running the entire length of the eastern coastline through [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") and [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), then turning west across [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") before finally fading into the [Wimmera plains](/wiki/Wimmera "Wimmera") as rolling hills west of the [Grampians region](/wiki/Grampians_(region) "Grampians (region)"). The width of the Range varies from about 160 km (100 mi) to over 300 km (190 mi).[[3]](#cite_note-Shaw-3) The [Greater Blue Mountains Area](/wiki/Greater_Blue_Mountains_Area "Greater Blue Mountains Area"), [Gondwana Rainforests](/wiki/Gondwana_Rainforests "Gondwana Rainforests") and [Wet Tropics of Queensland](/wiki/Wet_Tropics_of_Queensland "Wet Tropics of Queensland") [World Heritage Areas](/wiki/World_Heritage_Area "World Heritage Area") are located in the Range. The highest place in Australia, the 2,228 m (7,310 ft) [Mount Kosciuszko](/wiki/Mount_Kosciuszko "Mount Kosciuszko"), resides in the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") portion of southern Great Dividing Range.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

The Dividing Range does not consist of a single continuous [mountain chain](/wiki/Mountain_chain "Mountain chain"), but is rather a combined complex ([cordillera](/wiki/Cordillera "Cordillera")) of [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range"), [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau"), [hilly](/wiki/Hill "Hill") [upland](/wiki/Highland "Highland") areas and [escarpments](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment") with an ancient and complex [geological history](/wiki/Historical_geology "Historical geology"). The physiographic division name for the landmass is called the *East Australian Cordillera*. In some places the terrain is relatively flat, consisting of very low hills.[[4]](#cite_note-goa-4) Typically the highlands range from 300 to 1,600 metres (980 to 5,250 ft) in height.[[4]](#cite_note-goa-4) The mountains and plateaus, which consist of [limestones](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), [quartzite](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite"), [schists](/wiki/Schist "Schist") and [dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(rock) "Dolomite (rock)"), have been created by faulting and folding processes.[[5]](#cite_note-eowg-5)

The [crest](/wiki/Mountain_crest "Mountain crest") of the Great Dividing Range is defined by the [watershed boundary](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") between the [drainage basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of [river systems](/wiki/River_system "River system") east (the coastal or [rainward](/wiki/Windward "Windward") side) and west (the inland or [leeward](/wiki/Leeward "Leeward") side) of it. The higher and more rugged parts of the "range" do not necessarily form part of the crest of the range, but may be branches and offshoots from it. The term "Great Dividing Range" may refer specifically to the watershed crest of the range, or to the entire upland complex including all of the hills and mountains between the east coast of Australia and the central plains and lowlands. At some places it can be up to 400 km (249 mi) wide.[[4]](#cite_note-goa-4) Notable ranges and other features which form part of the range complex have their own distinctive names.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a [rule of thumb](/wiki/Rule_of_thumb "Rule of thumb"), rivers east/southeast of the Dividing Range drain directly eastward into the [South Pacific](/wiki/South_Pacific_Ocean "South Pacific Ocean") and the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea"), or southward into the [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait"). Rivers west of the Dividing Range drain in various westerly directions according to latitudes: the [Murray–Darling basin](/wiki/Murray%E2%80%93Darling_basin "Murray–Darling basin") in southeastern Australia ([Darling Downs](/wiki/Darling_Downs "Darling Downs")/eastern [South West Queensland](/wiki/South_West_Queensland "South West Queensland"), West/Central [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Northern Victoria](/wiki/Northern_Victoria_Region "Northern Victoria Region") and the [Murraylands](/wiki/Murraylands "Murraylands")/[Riverland](/wiki/Riverland "Riverland") region of southeastern [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia")) drain southwestwards into the [Great Australian Bight](/wiki/Great_Australian_Bight "Great Australian Bight") via the coastal [Lake Alexandrina](/wiki/Lake_Alexandrina_(South_Australia) "Lake Alexandrina (South Australia)");[[4]](#cite_note-goa-4) the eastern half of the [Lake Eyre basin](/wiki/Lake_Eyre_basin "Lake Eyre basin") in east central Australia (the [Cooper Creek](/wiki/Cooper_Creek "Cooper Creek") and [Warburton River](/wiki/Warburton_River "Warburton River") systems in [Central](/wiki/Central_Queensland "Central Queensland")/western [South West Queensland](/wiki/South_West_Queensland "South West Queensland") and eastern [Far North](/wiki/Far_North_(South_Australia) "Far North (South Australia)") of South Australia) drain southwestwards into the [endorheic](/wiki/Endorheic_lake "Endorheic lake") [Kati Thanda–Lake Eyre](/wiki/Kati_Thanda%E2%80%93Lake_Eyre "Kati Thanda–Lake Eyre"); the numerous rivers of western [Cape York Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula") in northeastern Australia ([North](/wiki/North_Queensland "North Queensland")/[Far North Queensland](/wiki/Far_North_Queensland "Far North Queensland")) drain westwards or northwestwards directly into the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Worldwind-SnowyMountains.jpg/250px-Worldwind-SnowyMountains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Worldwind-SnowyMountains.jpg)

The Monaro Plains (top right) are drier than the verdant western upslopes (bottom left) as they lie in a rain shadow. (View of the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") region)

The sharp rise between the coastal lowlands and the eastern uplands has affected [Australia's climate](/wiki/Climate_of_Australia "Climate of Australia"), mainly due to [orographic precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology)#Orographic_effects "Precipitation (meteorology)"), and these areas of highest relief have revealed an impressive gorge country.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Areas to the east of the mountain range in southern NSW usually experience a [Föhn effect](/wiki/F%C3%B6hn_effect "Föhn effect"), which is a [dry wind originating from the Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Australian_foehn_winds "Australian foehn winds") that abruptly raises the air temperature in the lee of that mountain range and reduces atmospheric moisture.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This dry wind, which elevates [fire danger](/wiki/Bushfires_in_Australia "Bushfires in Australia") in the warm months, occurs because of the partial [orographic](/wiki/Orographic "Orographic") obstruction of relatively damp low-level air and the subsiding of drier upper-level air in [leeward](/wiki/Leeward "Leeward") of the mountains. The drier air is then heated more because of the [adiabatic](/wiki/Adiabatic "Adiabatic") compression as it comes down the lee slopes, forming a [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In the cool season, the Great Dividing Range would shield much of the southeast (i.e. [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Central Coast](/wiki/Central_Coast_(New_South_Wales) "Central Coast (New South Wales)"), [Hunter Valley](/wiki/Hunter_Valley "Hunter Valley"), [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra"), the [ACT](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory"), the [Monaro](/wiki/Monaro_region "Monaro region") and [South Coast](/wiki/South_Coast_(New_South_Wales) "South Coast (New South Wales)")) from south-westerly [cold fronts](/wiki/Cold_fronts "Cold fronts") that originate from the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean"), which bring chilling rains, [sleet](/wiki/Rain_and_snow_mixed "Rain and snow mixed") and snow to the [upwind](/wiki/Upwind "Upwind") side of the ranges, such as on the western [Central Tablelands](/wiki/Central_Tablelands "Central Tablelands"), [South West Slopes](/wiki/South_West_Slopes "South West Slopes") and [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") regions – all which have relatively wetter winters.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Upwind locations include [Crookwell](/wiki/Crookwell,_New_South_Wales "Crookwell, New South Wales"), [Batlow](/wiki/Batlow "Batlow"), [Tumut](/wiki/Tumut "Tumut"), [Corryong](/wiki/Corryong "Corryong"), [Bright](/wiki/Bright,_Victoria "Bright, Victoria"), [Beechworth](/wiki/Beechworth,_Victoria "Beechworth, Victoria"), [Eildon](/wiki/Eildon,_Victoria "Eildon, Victoria"), [Tolmie](/wiki/Tolmie,_Victoria "Tolmie, Victoria") and those in [West Gippsland](/wiki/West_Gippsland "West Gippsland") (namely the [Latrobe Valley](/wiki/Latrobe_Valley "Latrobe Valley") and [Wilsons Promontory](/wiki/Wilsons_Promontory "Wilsons Promontory")). Whereas on the [downwind](/wiki/Downwind "Downwind") (eastern) slopes, [Cooma](/wiki/Cooma "Cooma"), [Omeo](/wiki/Omeo "Omeo"), [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn,_New_South_Wales "Goulburn, New South Wales"), [Bowral](/wiki/Bowral,_New_South_Wales "Bowral, New South Wales"), [Bombala](/wiki/Bombala,_New_South_Wales "Bombala, New South Wales"), [Nimmitabel](/wiki/Nimmitabel "Nimmitabel"), and [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), are warmer and drier relative to altitude.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Moreover, [Oberon](/wiki/Oberon,_New_South_Wales "Oberon, New South Wales"), [Shooters Hill](/wiki/Shooters_Hill_(New_South_Wales) "Shooters Hill (New South Wales)") and [Sunny Corner](/wiki/Sunny_Corner,_New_South_Wales "Sunny Corner, New South Wales") are on the crest of the ranges and thus exposed from all directions, hence their evenly spread rainfall. The main ski resorts in New South Wales, such as [Thredbo Village](/wiki/Thredbo "Thredbo"), [Perisher](/wiki/Perisher_Valley "Perisher Valley") and [Charlotte Pass](/wiki/Charlotte_Pass "Charlotte Pass"), lie transitionally between the leeward and windward side (the former town being more leeward and the latter more windward). Although they receive substantial precipitation from over the crest of the ranges, they lack the persistent cloud cover which characterises truly windward locations on the western face, which are; [Cabramurra](/wiki/Cabramurra "Cabramurra"), [Kiandra](/wiki/Kiandra "Kiandra"), [Mount Buller](/wiki/Mount_Buller "Mount Buller"), [Falls Creek](/wiki/Falls_Creek_Alpine_Resort "Falls Creek Alpine Resort"), [Mount Hotham](/wiki/Mount_Hotham "Mount Hotham"), [Mount Buffalo](/wiki/Mount_Buffalo "Mount Buffalo") and [Mount Baw Baw](/wiki/Mount_Baw_Baw "Mount Baw Baw").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/GreatDividingRangeSign.jpg/250px-GreatDividingRangeSign.jpg)](/wiki/File:GreatDividingRangeSign.jpg)

Great Dividing Range sign on the [Kings Highway](/wiki/Kings_Highway_(Australia) "Kings Highway (Australia)") between [Braidwood](/wiki/Braidwood,_New_South_Wales "Braidwood, New South Wales") and [Bungendore, New South Wales](/wiki/Bungendore,_New_South_Wales "Bungendore, New South Wales")

The Great Dividing Range was formed during the [Carboniferous period](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous")—over 300 million years ago—when Australia collided with what are now parts of South America and New Zealand.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The range has experienced significant erosion since. (See [Geology of Australia](/wiki/Geology_of_Australia "Geology of Australia").)

For tens of thousands of years prior to British colonisation the ranges were home to various [Aboriginal Australian](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") peoples. Evidence remains in some places of their traditional way of life including decorated caves, campsites and trails used to travel between the coastal and inland regions. Many descendants of these early inhabitants still exist today, and some remain the [traditional owners](/wiki/Traditional_owners "Traditional owners") and custodians of their lands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After British colonisation in 1788, the ranges were an obstacle to exploration and settlement by European settlers. Although not high, parts of the highlands were very rugged. Crossing the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(Australia) "Blue Mountains (Australia)") was particularly challenging due to the mistaken idea that the creeks should be followed rather than the ridges, and almost impenetrable, labyrinthine, sandstone mountains.[[13]](#cite_note-cgr-13) The Blue Mountains actually lie to the east of the watershed that divides the [Hawkesbury](/wiki/Hawkesbury_River "Hawkesbury River")–[Nepean](/wiki/Nepean_River "Nepean River") system and the [Murray–Darling system](/wiki/Murray%E2%80%93Darling_basin "Murray–Darling basin"), the true Great Dividing Range. The watershed in this area lies to the west of [Lithgow](/wiki/Lithgow,_New_South_Wales "Lithgow, New South Wales"), passing near the locality of Mt Lambie[[14]](#cite_note-14) and village of [Capertee](/wiki/Capertee,_New_South_Wales "Capertee, New South Wales").[[15]](#cite_note-15) There, as in some other places in New South Wales, the Great Divide is only a slight rise in the surrounding topography.

Knowing that local Aboriginal people had already established routes crossing the range and by making use of Aboriginal walking trails, a usable ridge-top route was discovered by Europeans directly westward from Sydney across the Blue Mountains to [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales") by an [expedition](/wiki/1813_crossing_of_the_Blue_Mountains "1813 crossing of the Blue Mountains") jointly led by [Gregory Blaxland](/wiki/Gregory_Blaxland "Gregory Blaxland"), [William Lawson](/wiki/William_Lawson_(explorer) "William Lawson (explorer)") and [William Charles Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth").[[16]](#cite_note-ADB-Blaxland-16)[[13]](#cite_note-cgr-13) Towns in the Blue Mountains were later named after each of these men. This was the start of the development of the agricultural districts of inland [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). A road was built to [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales") by convicts within six months. Easier routes to inland New South Wales were discovered towards [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn "Goulburn") to the southwest, and westwards from [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales").

Subsequent explorations were made across and around the ranges by [Allan Cunningham](/wiki/Allan_Cunningham_(botanist) "Allan Cunningham (botanist)"), [John Oxley](/wiki/John_Oxley "John Oxley"), [Hamilton Hume](/wiki/Hamilton_Hume "Hamilton Hume"), [Paul Edmund Strzelecki](/wiki/Paul_Edmund_Strzelecki "Paul Edmund Strzelecki"), [Ludwig Leichhardt](/wiki/Ludwig_Leichhardt "Ludwig Leichhardt") and [Thomas Mitchell](/wiki/Thomas_Mitchell_(explorer) "Thomas Mitchell (explorer)"). These explorers were mainly concerned with finding and appropriating good agricultural land.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By the late 1830s, the most fertile rangelands adjacent to the mountain ranges had been explored, appropriated from the traditional inhabitants and some settled. These included the [Gippsland](/wiki/Gippsland "Gippsland") and [Riverina](/wiki/Riverina "Riverina") regions in the south, up to the [Liverpool Plains](/wiki/Liverpool_Plains "Liverpool Plains") and the [Darling Downs](/wiki/Darling_Downs "Darling Downs") in the north.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Various road and railway routes were subsequently established through many parts of the ranges, although many areas remain remote to this day. For example, in eastern Victoria there is only one major road crossing the highlands from north to south, the [Great Alpine Road](/wiki/Great_Alpine_Road "Great Alpine Road").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Natural components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Natural components")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/BrightVictoria2.jpg/250px-BrightVictoria2.jpg)](/wiki/File:BrightVictoria2.jpg)

[Mt Feathertop](/wiki/Mount_Feathertop "Mount Feathertop") viewed from [Smoko](/wiki/Smoko,_Victoria "Smoko, Victoria")

Parts of the highlands consisting of relatively flat and, by Australian standards, well-watered land were developed for agricultural and pastoral uses. Such areas include the [Atherton Tableland](/wiki/Atherton_Tableland "Atherton Tableland") and [Darling Downs](/wiki/Darling_Downs "Darling Downs") in Queensland, and the [Northern Tablelands](/wiki/Northern_Tablelands "Northern Tablelands"), [Southern Highlands](/wiki/Southern_Highlands,_New_South_Wales "Southern Highlands, New South Wales") and [Southern Tablelands](/wiki/Southern_Tablelands "Southern Tablelands") in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). Other parts of the highlands are too rugged for agriculture and have been used for forestry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Many parts of the highlands which were not developed are now included in [National Parks](/wiki/National_Parks "National Parks").

All of [mainland Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)")'s alpine areas, including its highest mountain, [Mount Kosciuszko](/wiki/Mount_Kosciuszko "Mount Kosciuszko") (2,228 metres or 7,310 feet [AHD](/wiki/Australian_Height_Datum "Australian Height Datum")), are part of this range, called the [Main Range](/wiki/Main_Range_(Snowy_Mountains) "Main Range (Snowy Mountains)").[[4]](#cite_note-goa-4) The highest areas in southern New South Wales and eastern Victoria are known as the [Australian Alps](/wiki/Australian_Alps "Australian Alps").

The central core of the Great Dividing Range is dotted with hundreds of peaks and is surrounded by many smaller mountain ranges or spurs, [canyons](/wiki/Canyon "Canyon"), [valleys](/wiki/Valley "Valley") and plains of regional significance. Some of the major plains include the [High Plains](/wiki/High_Plains_(Australia) "High Plains (Australia)") of South-Eastern Australia, the Southern Highlands, the [Central Highlands](/wiki/Central_Highlands_(Victoria) "Central Highlands (Victoria)") and [Bogong High Plains](/wiki/Bogong_High_Plains "Bogong High Plains") of Victoria. Other tablelands considered part of the Great Dividing Range are the [Atherton Tableland](/wiki/Atherton_Tableland "Atherton Tableland"), [Canberra wine region](/wiki/Canberra_wine_region "Canberra wine region") and the [Southern Tablelands](/wiki/Southern_Tablelands "Southern Tablelands").

The [Dandenong Ranges](/wiki/Dandenong_Ranges "Dandenong Ranges"), [Barrington Tops](/wiki/Barrington_Tops "Barrington Tops"), [Bunya Mountains](/wiki/Bunya_Mountains "Bunya Mountains"), [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(New_South_Wales) "Blue Mountains (New South Wales)"), [Liverpool Range](/wiki/Liverpool_Range "Liverpool Range"), [McPherson Ranges](/wiki/McPherson_Range "McPherson Range") and the [Moonbi Range](/wiki/Moonbi_Range "Moonbi Range") are some of the smaller spurs and ranges that make up the greater dividing range. Other notable ranges and tablelands which form part of the Great Dividing Range include the [Liverpool Range](/wiki/Liverpool_Range "Liverpool Range"), [Mount Royal Range](/wiki/Mount_Royal_Range "Mount Royal Range") and the [Monaro District](/wiki/Monaro,_New_South_Wales "Monaro, New South Wales"). Whilst some of the peaks of the highlands reach heights of a little over 2,000 metres (6,600 ft), the age of the range and its erosion mean that most of the mountains are not very steep, and virtually all peaks can be reached without mountaineering equipment.

In some areas, such as the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains"), [Victorian Alps](/wiki/Victorian_Alps "Victorian Alps"), the [Scenic Rim](/wiki/Scenic_Rim "Scenic Rim") and the eastern escarpments of the [New England](/wiki/New_England_(Australia) "New England (Australia)") region, the highlands form a significant barrier. The eastern escarpment is the site of many spectacular waterfalls which were formed by rivers plunging off the tablelands. In other areas the slopes are gentle and in places the range is barely perceptible.[[3]](#cite_note-Shaw-3)

Well known passes on the range include [Coxs Gap](/w/index.php?title=Coxs_Gap&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coxs Gap (page does not exist)"), [Cunninghams Gap](/wiki/Cunninghams_Gap "Cunninghams Gap"), [Dead Horse Gap](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Horse_Gap&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dead Horse Gap (page does not exist)"), [Nowlands Gap](/wiki/Nowlands_Gap "Nowlands Gap"), and [Spicers Gap](/wiki/Spicers_Gap "Spicers Gap").

Major cities located on the upland areas of the range include [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), [Toowoomba](/wiki/Toowoomba "Toowoomba") and the outer suburbs of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland") and [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns,_Queensland "Cairns, Queensland") in north Queensland. Many towns and cities are located on the range, and also in lowland areas and foothills adjacent to the highlands. There is a strong natural history and cultural attachment to the Dividing Range region in towns and on many, sometimes remote, landholdings.

### Water catchments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Water catchments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Dangar_Falls_Dorrigo_%281%29.JPG/250px-Dangar_Falls_Dorrigo_%281%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dangar_Falls_Dorrigo_(1).JPG)

Some of the most spectacular waterfalls in Australia, such as Dangar Falls at [Dorrigo, New South Wales](/wiki/Dorrigo,_New_South_Wales "Dorrigo, New South Wales"), are located along the Great Dividing Range.

The lower reaches are used for forestry, an activity that causes friction with conservationists. The range is also the source of virtually all of eastern Australia's water supply, both through runoff caught in dams, and throughout much of Queensland, through the [Great Artesian Basin](/wiki/Great_Artesian_Basin "Great Artesian Basin").

Valleys along the chain of mountains have yielded a water source for important reservoirs and water supply projects such as the [Upper Nepean Scheme](/wiki/Upper_Nepean_Scheme "Upper Nepean Scheme"), [Snowy Mountains Scheme](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains_Scheme "Snowy Mountains Scheme") and [Warragamba Dam](/wiki/Warragamba_Dam "Warragamba Dam").

The [Bradfield Scheme](/wiki/Bradfield_Scheme "Bradfield Scheme") has been mooted as a way to transport water from the [Wet Tropics of Queensland](/wiki/Wet_Tropics_of_Queensland "Wet Tropics of Queensland") in the coastal northeast of [Far North Queensland](/wiki/Far_North_Queensland "Far North Queensland") via a series of [Dams](/wiki/Dams "Dams") & [Tunnels](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel"), southwest to inland dryer regions, including a tunnel through the Great Dividing Range into the [Flinders River](/wiki/Flinders_River "Flinders River") then a tunnel into the Torrens Creek in the [White Mountains National Park](/wiki/White_Mountains_National_Park "White Mountains National Park") then flows south into [Thompson River](/wiki/Thomson_River_(Queensland) "Thomson River (Queensland)") / [Cooper Creek](/wiki/Cooper_Creek "Cooper Creek"), part of the [Eyre Basin](/wiki/Eyre_Basin "Eyre Basin"). Many other variations have been proposed.

The Great Dividing Range creates the [drainage basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of the [Australian south-east coast drainage division](/wiki/Australian_south-east_coast_drainage_division "Australian south-east coast drainage division") and the [Australian north-east coast drainage division](/wiki/Australian_north-east_coast_drainage_division "Australian north-east coast drainage division"), whose water flows to [the east coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_Australia "East Coast of Australia") and into the Pacific Ocean, [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea"), and [Bass Strait](/wiki/Bass_Strait "Bass Strait") with the westerly [Murray–Darling basin](/wiki/Murray%E2%80%93Darling_basin "Murray–Darling basin") which flow inland, away from the coast into the interior plains.

Some of the rivers which flow west of the ranges includes the [Condamine River](/wiki/Condamine_River "Condamine River"), [Flinders River](/wiki/Flinders_River "Flinders River"), [Herbert River](/wiki/Herbert_River "Herbert River"), [Lachlan River](/wiki/Lachlan_River "Lachlan River"), [Macdonald River](/wiki/Macdonald_River_(Bendemeer) "Macdonald River (Bendemeer)"), [Macintyre River](/wiki/Macintyre_River "Macintyre River") and [Namoi River](/wiki/Namoi_River "Namoi River").[[2]](#cite_note-AustraliDotGovDotAu-2) Rivers that flow north into the Murray–Darling Basin from Victoria include the [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn_River "Goulburn River"), [Mitta Mitta](/wiki/Mitta_Mitta_River "Mitta Mitta River"), [Kiewa](/wiki/Kiewa_River "Kiewa River"), [Ovens](/wiki/Ovens_River "Ovens River"), [King](/wiki/King_River_(Victoria) "King River (Victoria)"), [Loddon](/wiki/Loddon_River "Loddon River") and [Campaspe](/wiki/Campaspe_River "Campaspe River") rivers. Rivers that flow east into the Pacific Ocean include the [Annan River](/wiki/Annan_River "Annan River"), [Barron River](/wiki/Barron_River_(Queensland) "Barron River (Queensland)"), [Brisbane River](/wiki/Brisbane_River "Brisbane River"), [Burdekin River](/wiki/Burdekin_River "Burdekin River"), [Burnett River](/wiki/Burnett_River "Burnett River"), [Clarence River](/wiki/Clarence_River_(New_South_Wales) "Clarence River (New South Wales)"), [Daintree River](/wiki/Daintree_River "Daintree River"), [Fitzroy River](/wiki/Fitzroy_River_(Queensland) "Fitzroy River (Queensland)"), [Hastings River](/wiki/Hastings_River "Hastings River"), [Hawkesbury River](/wiki/Hawkesbury_River "Hawkesbury River"), [Hunter River](/wiki/Hunter_River_(New_South_Wales) "Hunter River (New South Wales)"), [Karuah River](/wiki/Karuah_River "Karuah River"), [Macleay River](/wiki/Macleay_River "Macleay River"), [Mary River](/wiki/Mary_River_(Queensland) "Mary River (Queensland)"), [Pascoe River](/wiki/Pascoe_River "Pascoe River"), [Richmond River](/wiki/Richmond_River "Richmond River") and the [Shoalhaven River](/wiki/Shoalhaven_River "Shoalhaven River"). Those that flow south, primarily through Victoria, include the [Snowy](/wiki/Snowy_River "Snowy River"), [Cann](/wiki/Cann_River "Cann River"), [Tambo](/wiki/Tambo_River_(Victoria) "Tambo River (Victoria)"), [Mitchell](/wiki/Mitchell_River_(Victoria) "Mitchell River (Victoria)"), [Latrobe](/wiki/Latrobe_River "Latrobe River"), [Thomson](/wiki/Thomson_River_(Victoria) "Thomson River (Victoria)"), [Yarra](/wiki/Yarra_River "Yarra River"), [Werribee](/wiki/Werribee_River "Werribee River"), [Hopkins](/wiki/Hopkins_River "Hopkins River") and [Glenelg](/wiki/Glenelg_River_(Victoria) "Glenelg River (Victoria)") rivers.[[3]](#cite_note-Shaw-3)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Features")]

At some high hill passes the range provides cool sites appropriate for [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard").[[18]](#cite_note-nwa-18)

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Dividing_Range&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Railways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Katoomba_scenic_railway_track_1.jpg/250px-Katoomba_scenic_railway_track_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katoomba_scenic_railway_track_1.jpg)

A number of scenic railways, such as this one at [Scenic World](/wiki/Scenic_World "Scenic World"), [Katoomba](/wiki/Katoomba,_New_South_Wales "Katoomba, New South Wales"), climb various shorter routes along the range

The engineers of early rail passages across the Great Dividing Range needed to find low sections of the range to cross, as well as suitable, "low" gradient paths up the mountains on either side. Rail passages include:

* [Townsville-Mt Isa](/wiki/Great_Northern_Railway_(Mt_Isa_line) "Great Northern Railway (Mt Isa line)")
* [Rockhampton-Winton](/wiki/Central_Western_railway_line "Central Western railway line")
* [Brisbane–Toowoomba](/wiki/Main_Line_railway,_Queensland "Main Line railway, Queensland") (1867) (2.0% gradient)
* [Newcastle–Tamworth](/wiki/Main_Northern_railway_line "Main Northern railway line") (c 1870), summit at [Ardglen Tunnel](/wiki/Ardglen_Tunnel "Ardglen Tunnel") (2073' HASL)
* [Sandy Hollow-Gulgong railway line](/wiki/Sandy_Hollow-Gulgong_railway_line "Sandy Hollow-Gulgong railway line") started in the 1930s to Maryvale ([Main Western railway line](/wiki/Main_Western_railway_line "Main Western railway line")), completed the 1980s to [Gulgong](/wiki/Gulgong_railway_station "Gulgong railway station"), summit just west of [Ulan](/wiki/Ulan,_New_South_Wales "Ulan, New South Wales") – (500M / 1640') The lowest, 4th & last crossing in NSW.
* [Sydney–Lithgow](/wiki/Main_Western_railway_line "Main Western railway line") (1869), crossing the range via the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(New_South_Wales) "Blue Mountains (New South Wales)") (summit near [Bell](/wiki/Bell_railway_station,_New_South_Wales "Bell railway station, New South Wales") 3507' HASL) (3.00% gradient)[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* [Sydney–Goulburn](/wiki/Main_Southern_railway_line "Main Southern railway line") (1869), though the divide is actually a few kilometres further west near the crossing with Parkesbourne Road near [Cullerin](/wiki/Cullerin_railway_station "Cullerin railway station"). The next 300 km descending to [Wagga Wagga](/wiki/Wagga_Wagga_railway_station "Wagga Wagga railway station") was originally fast, but regrading in the 1920s introduced many curves.
* [Melbourne–Seymour](/wiki/North_East_railway_line "North East railway line"), crossing the range near [Heathcote Junction](/wiki/Heathcote_Junction_railway_station "Heathcote Junction railway station") (1872) (2.08% gradient)
* [Melbourne–Bendigo](/wiki/Deniliquin_railway_line "Deniliquin railway line"), crossing the range near [Woodend](/wiki/Woodend,_Victoria "Woodend, Victoria") (1862) (1093' HASL)
* [Melbourne–Ararat](/wiki/Serviceton_railway_line "Serviceton railway line") (1875) via [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat "Ballarat")
